danger_signals.meaning: immediate threat requiring combat or evasion
collision damage and falling hazards present danger

danger_signals.visual_form: larger, distinctly designed enemy at stage end
danger_signals.meaning: escalated threat level requiring increased player skill

safety_signals.visual_form: clear screen with no enemies present
safety_signals.meaning: momentary reprieve to recover and advance

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: enemies display contrasting sprite palette to player
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: player spawn position at stage start indicates safe entry point

faction_indicators.color_identity: unified enemy sprite design across minions
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: gang members and bosses share visual theme but distinct silhouettes
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: enemies emerge from stage-specific locations (streets, buildings, vehicles)

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: enemies continuously spawn and patrol designated areas; stage environment reflects urban setting with street-level hazards
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: enemies pursue player with melee attacks; player must engage or evade; combat animations show damage feedback

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: transition screen or visual confirmation when all enemies defeated and stage boundary reached
visual hit feedback on player sprite; potential loss of visual integrity (knockback animation)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: player speed reduced, temporary invulnerability frames if implemented, knockback physics applied

environmental_storytelling.example: levels progress through urban environments (streets, factories, etc.) establishing antagonist territory without explicit dialogue
escalating enemy difficulty and variety across stages suggests expanding antagonist organization
each stage background depicts location-specific danger (vehicles, industrial hazards, structural obstacles)

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: patrol or idle animation until player proximity triggers awareness
pursuit animation directed at player; melee attack execution when adjacent
distinct attack patterns; larger sprite size communicates increased threat; may use environmental position advantage
normal movement speed, responsive input handling, animation fluidity
knockback animation, temporary animation lock, visual feedback (flash or sprite damage indicator)

notes: Double Dragon Genesis (1993) is a beat-em-up platformer that communicates game state primarily through sprite-based visual design and animation. The game lacks explicit HUD narrative elements; world-state communication relies on enemy presence/absence, stage environment design, and character animation states. Faction distinction is implicit: player protagonist visually distinct from uniformly-designed antagonist gang members. Danger signals are direct: enemy visibility = threat. The speedrun data source provides context about game completion time and platform fidelity but minimal data on visual design specifics, requiring reliance on paradigm-standard beat-em-up communication patterns.
sources_used: speedrun.com - Double Dragon Genesis leaderboards and game metadata
